zotero.abstractNote: Object-centric learning aims to decompose scenes into individual objects, which is hypothesized to be a key step towards human-level intelligence. While recent methods have shown impressive results in scene decomposition, their impact on downstream tasks remains under-explored. In this work, we systematically evaluate the generalization and robustness of object-centric models on a variety of downstream tasks. We find that object-centric models do not consistently outperform standard monolithic architectures. In fact, we identify a trade-off between scene decomposition quality and downstream performance. We further show that object-centric models are not inherently more robust to distribution shifts than monolithic models. Our results suggest that current object-centric models may not yet be capturing the right inductive biases for general-purpose representation learning.
